summary: DHH argues that targeted advertising should be banned by law, citing the striking statistic that 96% of US iOS users opted out of app tracking when Apple gave them the choice in iOS 14.5. He contends this near-unanimous rejection demolishes the ad industry's claim that people welcome tracking for 'relevant ads.' Beyond privacy, he argues that banning targeted ads would dismantle surveillance capitalism and restore value to quality content creation, which has been reduced to commodity-level 'engagement' metrics by the Google-Facebook ad duopoly.
key_points: 96% of US iOS users opted out of app tracking when given the choice, proving that earlier industry polls showing only 60% opposition were misleading
Banning targeted ads would cut off funding for surveillance capitalism since major brands would stop buying stalker ads if they were illegal
Targeted advertising has commodified all content by measuring worth purely in 'engagement,' devastating journalism and quality publishing
The near-unanimous public rejection of tracking constitutes a democratic mandate for legislative action, not just platform-level opt-outs
money_quotes: The American public can barely agree on anything, but their dislike of targeted ads appear damn near unanimous.
Ban targeted ads entirely. Not just as a feature of one of the two dominant mobile operating systems, but entirely, and by law.

If Facebook and Google couldn't turn the worst sludge of the internet into gold through the alchemy of targeted ads, much of that gold would return to its rightful owners: the people creating great stuff.
Targeted advertisement has reduced all content to commodities, which has its worth measured purely in 'engagement'.
That nasty, viral post on Facebook is worth just as much as that deeply-researched magazine article, as long as the same number of people spend the same amount of time on it.
But the polls were wrong (probably because they were rigged!). The results from reality are in. 96% don't. NINETY SIX PERCENT.
That's not just a landslide election, that's a mandate. A fucking obligation.
key_insight: The 96% opt-out rate on iOS tracking proves that targeted advertising survives not because people want it, but because they were never given a real choice — and the democratic response should be an outright legislative ban.
